sentences.text: Well, the redhead said, no, we won't let him go.
We'll investigate in his hometown, where it is politically neutral.
They suspected me, really, of sabotage and espionage.
And I could gather from their-- from their-- from their-- because they looked into my documents.
I have no documents.
So when I heard this, they want to investigate in my hometown-- of course, everything was false.
My name was false.
My hometown was false.
So I saw no way out.
I saw the end of the rope.
And I decided to commit suicide that night.
I remember, they were giving out-- they were giving out these so-called black coffee with a slice of bread every morning.
The coffee was not coffee.
It was black water, really.
And I had still my shoelaces, leather shoe laces.
They didn't take it away from me.
They thought you cannot hang yourself with it.
So they left me my leather shoelaces.
So I soaked shoelaces in that coffee.
And there was a pipe in the cell going from the bottom up and then across the ceiling.
I figured the pipe across the ceiling would be a perfect place to hang myself.
And there's-- there was also a little light over the door always peeking into the cell.
And in my cell was another friend of mine, a Polish fellow that was arrested together with me.
His name was [INAUDIBLE].
I still remember his name.
And I soaked these-- my shoelaces in that coffee, should be soft.
I should be able to hang myself.
And when he was sleeping already-- because we were sleeping on the floor.
When he was-- I thought he was sleeping-- I was very nervous that day.
I made this thing ready for to be hanged.
I tied it up, the shoelaces to the-- to the pipe.
And I was about to get my head into the sling.
But he was not sleeping.
He noticed-- something wrong about me that day.
He jumped up, crying.
He said, [?
Stefan, ?] what are you doing?
What, do you want to leave me alone here?
I couldn't even share with him my feelings.
I couldn't share with nobody my feelings.
I couldn't tell him my story, what my problem is.
I was all alone.
But I saw a sign from heaven.
Apparently I have to live.
If he didn't let me to hang myself, I definitely saw a sign from heaven that I have to survive.
But what do you do to survive?
And I was-- I was laying all night long on the floor, and thinking, what can I do, my next step to [INAUDIBLE] to survive?
And I thought I had this-- like a suburban coat, which I-- which I made just before I left for Germany.
And there was this so-called kalfaktor.
A kalfaktor was a privileged prisoner who was giving out food to the cells in the morning, noon time, and in the evening.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] His name was Hans.
And he came in the morning.
I said to him, Hans, would you like my suburban coat?
He said, yes.
And I said, I'll tell you what.
All you have to do is get me a piece of paper with a pencil with an envelope.
I will write a letter.
But this letter has to be registered.
It's called einschreiben.
And you have to give me a receipt that the letter was mailed, which I know he knew all the guards in the-- because he was a privileged prisoner, he knew all the guards.
So I know he would make contact with me.
He said, what?
That's all you want?
I said, yes.
I'll tell you-- I'll give you double portions every day too.
I said, [INAUDIBLE], if you give me double portions, you to give my friend [INAUDIBLE] also double portions.
So I gave him the thing.
He brought me he-- brought me the paper with the envelope.
And I wrote this letter to a Polish mason that I worked with him just before I left.
He befriended me.
His name was [INAUDIBLE].
I wrote him the letter.
And in this letter-- in Polish, of course-- I wrote in a hidden way he should know who I am and he should give this letter to my father in the ghetto.
I never knew-- I knew that he got the letter, because I got a receipt.
But what he did with this letter I never knew till after the war.
And I'll tell you later what happened.
He really gave this letter to my father.
My father went to discuss [INAUDIBLE] of mine of [INAUDIBLE].
He had some contacts.
And he went to the village where I suppose that I came from.
And as it turned out, the Ukrainian priest was the mayor of the village.
And he told him, if you get a call from the Gestapo about Stefan [?
Galushka, ?] tell him he was a quiet person, he went to work in Germany.
That's all.
And as it turned out, that call came through-- I found after the war.
And he gave-- and he gave me a good, good report.
But I never knew about it.
And I was always in fear-- what has happened?
How long-- for how much-- how much time were you in-- in that jail in--
It was about five weeks.
In Bochum, in [INAUDIBLE].

At that time, it came out, apparently-- it was-- I found out later that Hitler gave out an order that all these prisoners that are more than a month in-- under investigation, to ship them as political prisoners to concentration camp.
And before I know it, I found myself up on the train with, of course, with the police accompanying us.
And I wound up in concentration camp in Wewelsburg, because they call a concentration-- [GERMAN] Niederhagen Wewelsburg [GERMAN].
And there I wound up.
That was approximately the-- maybe February of '43.
It was February or March, something like that, of '43.
And--
Yeah.
I'll describe my arrival there.
We arrive in civilian uniforms.
The first thing, they took us to a bed.
They-- they-- they cut our hair.
We're triple zero.
They gave us striped uniforms.
Old civilian uniforms had to be given away.
All the possessions had to be given away.
We had nothing but the striped uniforms on us, with wooden shoes.
And that was cold.
When I came to-- they put us all into one barrack, so-called [INAUDIBLE]-- One barrack where all the newcomers cameras were there.
And the-- sometimes later, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the old prisoners came to visit us.
Everybody was looking-- maybe you find somebody who's your friend.
It was one guy-- it was an old prisoner.
As it turned out, his name was Willy Brozawski.
He was a German.
He had a black triangle.
I never knew why he was-- the black triangle's called-- they were called-- so-called [GERMAN]-- anti-social elements, [GERMAN].
And he was an old prisoner.
He hailed from East Prussia.
And he spoke Polish.
And he liked me, sort of.
And he told me, you know?
Your-- your-- your whole transport is designated to go to the Eastern Front to Russia to dig ditches.
And nobody ever came out alive from there.
So he asked me, what is your profession?
And I said, I'm a mason.
He says, you know, there's a shortage of masons here.
Tomorrow morning, there will be an appell.
Appell was the counting of the prisoners, standing in rows of five.
And they were counting if all the prisoners are there.
And there will the kommandant-- Hauptsturmfuhrer Haas was his name.
Go out after the appell.
In your broken German, tell him that you-- you-- you-- that you want to stay here in Wewelsburg and you're a mason.
And I listened to him, and I did.
And he-- he designated me I should stay there in Wewelsburg instead of being shipped to the for certain death.
So again, it was-- it was a lucky move.
And as it turned out, I was assigned to a block-- block one.
I remember the blockalteste-- his name was-- he was a-- he had a green-- a green triangle.
Green was the criminals, befouled, the [GERMAN].
Professional criminals were there too.
They were Germans.
His name was Fritz Herzog.
And he was pretty strict.
And I was assigned a bed there in that-- in that-- in that barrack.
And I was assigned to work in the kitchen to build-- to expand the kitchen.
There were a lot of Jehovah's Witnesses there in that camp.
As a matter of fact, the so-called [GERMAN], the main guy, was a Jehovah's Witness.
And as it turned out, my kapo who was-- who-- who I was assigned to, to work in the kitchen, to work in the kitchen to do masonry work, he was also a Jehovah's Witness.
His name was Adam Schmidt.
Still remember the name.
He sort of liked me, and he gave me work inside the kitchen, and that was a blessing in the wintertime to work inside the kitchen.
And on top of it, there were some raw vegetables, like carrots and things like that, that I could eat them on the side, that helped me too.
And I was in Wewelsburg-- I was there for a few months.
A lot of people were killed there, because there was-- there was a lot of stone-- there were stone quarries.
And most of the people were-- had to be-- had too much in the stone quarries.
And every day, a number of guys were killed-- either died from starvation or from dysentery or from beating.
And they had to carry him on the-- on their shoulders back to the camp.
And there was a lot of dysentery there.
People were dying like flies there from dysentery, from typhoid.
And I remember, to come back for a moment, when I just came to Wewelsburg, I smelled-- the chimney of the crematorium was apparently active.
I smelled a burning of like, of nails.
As it turned out, it was a crematorium, and they were burning all the dead-- dead prisoners.
I remember once, I had to work in the-- in the so-called [INAUDIBLE] in the-- [INAUDIBLE] was the hospital of the concentration camp.
And I had to do some masonry work.
And I went to the basement there.
And there were, in that [INAUDIBLE], there were stacks of dead prisoners, completely naked, with their-- with big numbers of theirs written on their bellies.
They were ready to be taken to the crematorium.
They were stacked like wood-- I mean, hundreds.
It's amazing enough that Wewelsburg had only a few thousand people.
It would be 4,000 men-- four or five at the most.
And a few hundred people, new people, were always brought in.
But there were never more-- a few hundred people out the window and into the camp to stabilize the number of the thing there.
In the camp?
No-- Niederhagen, no.
I was there for-- I was about-- about February '42.
'43, sorry.
I was there until late spring or early summer of '43.
They decided-- the Germans decided to convert Wewelsburg into a field hospital for the wounded SS from the Eastern Front.
And they vacated us with-- in the cattle-- cattle trains, cattle cars.
And we wound up in Ravensbrück.
Ravensbrück, by Furstenberg and Mecklenburg.
It was east of Berlin, northeast of Berlin.
And I wound up in Ravensbrück.
That was in the-- early-- late spring or early summer of '43.
Ravensbrück.
Ravensbrück was-- there was-- was a huge female camp.
About 75,000 women were there, mostly from all over Europe.
And there was a small-- relatively small-- small male camp, about 5,000 men, with five barracks there.
And I was assigned to that camp.
Of course, they registered me as a mason again.
And we were assigned to build, to expand the women's camp.
We built not only the camp.
We built also the called-- the so-called [GERMAN] textile factories.
They were-- they were doing textiles work for the-- for the war effort.
And also, we built factories for Siemens.
They're-- they're active now in this country now, in Germany too.
But they used slave labor.
We built factories for Siemens.
They're called Siemens and Halske-- and Ravensbrück, for the women to be employed as slave labor there.
I was-- at that time, I was about 19 years old.
About 19 years old.
And I have another question.
Did you know, at that time, at all what was happening with your family?
No, I was completely cut off.
I had no contact with them at all.
As a matter of fact, normally, people had contact-- normal prisoners.
They used to get, once a month, either a package or a letter from their hometown.
I was the only one who didn't get it.
But later on, I was lucky, through an incident, that I started getting packages-- namely, I was in a so-called kommando of masons, a group of masons.
And they put us to work in the female concentration camp to expand the kitchen, build chimneys.
Being that I was a master mason, I knew how to build chimneys.
So me and a group, maybe of 20 guys-- of course, we were surrounded by the SS.
They didn't let us have contact with the women-- and to expand the kitchen.
So we broke one wall.
So we had a total view of that kitchen.
And we worked on the scaffolds.
And from the scaffolds, I could see every what was going on in that kitchen.
And they were mostly Polish women working that kitchen-- prisoners.
And they were-- they were cooking stuff for the-- for the SS, for the guards, SS guards.
And I-- and I saw, I could see from the-- from the place where I was working on the--
Scaffold-- I saw they had vessels full of ground raw meat.
They have-- they have it ground with onions.
And the SS love-- liked very much raw meat ground to eat, just like this.
And I noticed that.
And we had to go off to get water for our-- for our-- for-- for the work, for the mortar, for-- to mix the mortar with, get water from the kitchen.
Of course, the SS was watching-- are we going, are we coming, and so forth.
I noticed a Polish woman.
And I-- I started, like, from a distance, flirting with her, and she flirted with me.
And as I was going to, with a pail, with two pails, to pick up water, and I saw a pail of meat standing there, I exchanged one pail-- two full of water, one pail of meat.
And they didn't notice it.
And I continued that practice for a while-- one pail of meat, one pail of water.
And I started not only eating myself, but feeding my whole group.
But after about two or three weeks, the SS noticed that we gained weight sort of.
They knew that something fishy was going on there.
And they took it out from there.
But in the meantime, I made contact with that Polish woman.
She knew my name already, my number, the block where-- the barrack where I lived, and so forth.
And she-- as it turned out, she wrote to her parents in Poland, that she would forego her packages if they'll send me the packages there, because she has enough food.
She works in the kitchen.

And one day, after the appell-- after the so-called appell, they call it, the-- at evening-- they always called out number-- always called numbers with people to pick up packages or for other reasons.
They called for the-- for instance, every day for another Jehovah's Witness, see if wants to resign from his faith, they will let him go.
They never called me.
One day, they're calling me.
I almost fainted, and thought, ah, they discovered me already.
They-- they got a letter from my hometown.
They're going to shoot and going to execute me.
And I'm going there.
They said, no, you have a package there-- pick up.
I am going there.
There's a big package.
I could hardly carry it.
I bring it to my-- to my block-- from her parents.
But and then soon enough, shortly after, I got a letter with regards asking me different questions.
I brought this-- this package had a double purpose for me.
Number one, it legitimized me.
I was also get-- also family.
Of course, I had to make up a story that why I don't get packages.
My-- my parent-- my parents were-- were deported to Siberia and they were shot and I was left.
But it was more-- more believable, that actually getting packages and getting letters.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was-- I started to live it up, because first of all, in that package, there was real good stuff.
They had even a stuffed-- a stuffed duck with apples inside, fat-- what do you call it?
What do you call this fat from pigs?
Lard.
Oh, lard.
Yes.
Lard, melted lard, bread cakes-- I started sharing this food with-- with a group of my friends, Poles, and they became very friendly with me.
And they-- they helped me.
They wanted-- did this for me, then did the other for me.
I became prominent.
And they-- I created like my own family with four or five guys, because I couldn't-- I couldn't possibly eat it myself, that they should stare at me and eat all that-- all that stuff.
And it was very, very helpful to me create a loyal-- a loyal grouping for me.
And I was getting them-- from that point on, I was getting them regularly, practically every month till about two months before the war ended.
And that was a great, great help to me.
Yes, I was employed all time as a mason.
Because there was a shortage of good masons, and I was a master mason.
So they tried to utilize me as a mason.
I mean, the fruit-- the food that they giving-- they were giving was starvation food.
It was-- a small, small, tiny slice of bread a day with a soup during the day with some-- it's called [GERMAN].
It was like beets, white beets, and some carrots sometimes swimming there.
It never did except for one instance, and one instance, with a kapo.
His name was Paul.
I don't remember his last name.
He wore that-- he had a black triangle.
He was an old timer.
So once he came-- I was working.
He came up to the scaffold.
And my group, my kommando, was mostly Polish guys.
He came to the scaffold.
He puts his foot on the-- on the back, where mortar was.
He says to me, Stefan, I suspect that you are a half a Jew.
Don't you have Jewish in your family?
This was the most critical moment of my life in Ravensbrück.
I couldn't think longer.
I said, what?
I grabbed him by the neck and I threw him down the scaffold.
To threw down the-- throw down the kapo off my scaffold was a certain death.
There was a-- he was an untouchable.
But I-- in desperation, I did it.
And to make sure, I pushed the-- some bricks in the mortar, also from the thing.
Everything fell down-- made it appear like it was an accident.
But he-- he himself knew it was no accident that I pushed him.
And I came down the scaffold.
All my friends, the Poles, came running to me.
What?
What's happened here?
I said, you know what he said?
He suspected me I'm a Jew.
Then they started beating up on him.
They were so upset that he dared to suspect me.
So from that moment on-- point on, that kapo never reported the incident.
He never did, lucky for me. but He avoided me like a plague.
Whenever he saw me, he just went the other way.
So that think was the most critical moment of my being in Germany.
If I would have acted differently, I would probably-- it would have been the end of me.
If I would have turned pale or something-- so that was that incident.
Let me just bring up another incident-- another incident, but it was a routine.
Every Saturday night, the whole block, the whole barrack, had to proceed to so-called [GERMAN].

Everybody had to strip naked, and the [GERMAN], so-called-- the charge of the, of the wing, of that, of that barrack-- checked for lice-- checked-- checked at the-- by the genitals, under the armpits, because there was-- there was a lot of lice.
Every Saturday night was a nightmare for me.
I figured, this is the end of me.
They'll-- tonight, they'll recognize.
Can you imagine how I felt every Saturday night?
But when I came for-- when my turn came there, I grabbed the end of my penis.
I pulled a little bit the foreskin over it, and they looked, and-- and they never-- they never suspected me, and they went through.
But my heart was at my elbow every time.
I figured this-- today will catch me.
But somehow, [INAUDIBLE] they never suspected me.
I played my role so well, there was-- there was not a slight suspicion that I could be a Jew.
That is why they probably didn't pay attention to it.
And I made it.

It was towards the end of April-- well, in '45, I would say it was March '45-- American airplanes were flying over our camp and throwing down these-- these metal strips and covering the whole camp with metal.
So we didn't know the-- the meaning of these metal strips.
But we knew these are Americans.
As a matter of fact, I was working at a kommando near Ravensbrück and from a-- from a high-- higher elevation, I could see Berlin.
We were not far from Berlin.
And you could see in broad daylight how American allied planes had been bombarding Berlin.
That was a sight to see.
We enjoyed tremendously-- we knew this is the end, that they were destroying the city of our-- of our-- where our torturers lived.
And we knew that the end of the war will come.
